title: The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich
subtitle: A History of Nazi Germany
author: William L. Shirer
authorBio: American journalist, war correspondent, and historian. One of the first reporters hired by Edward R. Murrow at CBS, Shirer covered Nazi Germany from 1925 to 1940, was present at the Anschluss and the Munich Agreement, and broadcast the invasion of France and the fall of Paris. After the war he used his access to captured Nazi documents and his own extensive diary notes to write this monumental history.
originalTitle: The Rise and Fall of the Third Reich: A History of Nazi Germany
editionNote: Simon & Schuster Reprint (1981). First published 1960 by Simon & Schuster, New York. The 1981 reprint carries a new foreword by the author. Original hardcover ran approximately 1,250 pages.
themes: The Rise of Dictatorship in a Democracy
The Role of Propaganda and Myth-Making
Resistance and the Cost of Courage
excerpt: Drawing on his own decade as a CBS correspondent in Nazi Berlin, captured Nazi government archives seized at war's end, and the Nuremberg trial records, William L. Shirer reconstructs the complete arc of Nazism — from the Weimar Republic's collapse through Hitler's rise, the consolidation of total terror, the descent into global war, the Holocaust, and the final destruction of Berlin in 1945. More than a chronicle of events, the book is an autopsy of a civilization that chose self-destruction: how propaganda, intimidation, bureaucratic complicity, and the Führer myth converted a highly educated European nation into an engine of industrialized murder. Published in 1960, it became a defining work of 20th-century history and remains the single most cited popular history of Nazi Germany.
